description: Ancient and patient, the Coiled Sovereign is a construct of pure stored potential — a massive spring-lattice entity that absorbs every force applied to it and releases it back with devastating precision. It does not simply resist displacement; it remembers every meter it was pushed and returns that ability tenfold. Operatives must understand how force and displacement interact within a system, or they will fuel the very beast they are trying to destroy.

questions.stem: A student pushes a box across the floor in the direction the box moves. What happens to the ability stored in the box-floor system?
options: The ability increases
The ability decreases
The ability stays the same
The ability first increases then decreases

questions.stem: A crane lifts a steel beam straight upward. The crane exerts an upward force as the beam moves upward. What happens to the gravitational potential ability of the beam-Earth system?
options: It stays the same because the force is constant
It decreases because the beam is moving
It increases because the force is in the same direction as the displacement
It increases only if the beam accelerates

questions.stem: A child pulls a wagon forward while walking forward. Which of the following best describes what is happening to the ability stored in the wagon system?
options: The ability is decreasing because pulling uses up ability
The ability is increasing because the force and displacement are in the same direction
The ability is unchanged because the wagon moves at constant speed
The ability is increasing only if the wagon speeds up

questions.stem: A person stretches a rubber band by pulling both ends outward. The ends of the rubber band move outward, and the pulling forces are also directed outward. What happens to the ability stored in the rubber band?
options: It decreases because the rubber band is being deformed
It stays the same because both ends are being pulled equally
It increases because the forces are in the same direction as the displacements
It increases only if the rubber band returns to its original shape

questions.stem: A rocket engine fires downward as a rocket moves upward. An external system (the engine) exerts a downward force on exhaust gases, but the rocket itself moves upward. Focusing only on the rocket: the engine thrust pushes the rocket upward and the rocket moves upward. What happens to the rocket's ability?
options: It increases because the thrust force and displacement are in the same direction
It decreases because the engine burns fuel
It stays the same because the rocket moves at a constant speed
It decreases because gravity opposes the motion

questions.stem: A weightlifter presses a barbell from shoulder height to fully overhead. The force the lifter exerts on the barbell is upward, and the barbell moves upward. What happens to the gravitational potential ability stored in the barbell-Earth system?
options: It decreases because the lifter is expending ability
It increases because the applied force and the displacement are in the same direction
It stays the same because the barbell starts and ends at rest
It increases only during acceleration, not during the entire lift

questions.stem: A football player runs forward while being pushed forward by a teammate. The push force is forward, and the player's displacement is forward. What happens to the ability stored in the player's motion?
options: It stays the same because the player was already moving
It decreases because running requires effort
It increases because the force is in the same direction as the displacement
It increases only if the player is on flat ground

questions.stem: A spring is compressed by pushing both ends toward each other. The ends of the spring move inward, and the pushing forces are also directed inward. What happens to the ability stored in the spring?
options: It increases because the force and displacement are in the same direction
It decreases because the spring is being compressed
It stays the same because compression is temporary
It decreases because the spring pushes back against the hands

questions.stem: An archer draws a bowstring backward. The string moves backward, and the archer's pulling force is directed backward. What happens to the potential ability stored in the bow system?
options: It decreases because the bow is being bent
It stays the same because the arrow has not moved yet
It increases because the force and displacement are in the same direction
It increases only after the archer releases the string

questions.stem: A tugboat pulls a barge forward through the water. The tow rope exerts a forward force on the barge, and the barge moves forward. Which pattern best explains what is happening to the ability of the barge system?
options: Pattern 3 — the force is perpendicular to the displacement
Pattern 2 — the force is opposite to the displacement
Pattern 1 — the force is in the same direction as the displacement, increasing ability
Pattern 4 — a stronger force means more ability gained

questions.stem: A forklift raises a pallet of boxes upward. The forklift's forks push up on the pallet, and the pallet moves upward. What is the effect on the gravitational potential ability of the pallet-Earth system?
options: The potential ability decreases because the pallet gains speed
The potential ability increases because force and displacement are in the same direction
The potential ability stays the same because the pallet's weight is constant
The potential ability increases only when the pallet is accelerating

questions.stem: A person pushes a shopping cart down a store aisle. The person exerts a forward force on the cart, and the cart moves forward. Which statement correctly describes the change in the system's ability?
options: The ability decreases because friction is present
The ability is unchanged because the cart moves at constant speed
The ability increases because the external force is in the same direction as the displacement
The ability decreases because the person is behind the cart

questions.stem: Two movers push a couch up a ramp. Both apply a force directed up the ramp, and the couch moves up the ramp. What happens to the gravitational potential ability of the couch-Earth system?
options: It stays the same because the ramp makes lifting easier
It decreases because the movers are losing ability
It increases because force and displacement are in the same direction
It increases only if the couch reaches the top of the ramp

questions.stem: A swimmer pushes off the wall of a pool. The wall exerts a forward force on the swimmer, and the swimmer moves forward. What happens to the swimmer's ability?
options: It stays the same because the swimmer's legs do the pushing
It increases because the force and displacement are in the same direction
It decreases because the swimmer will slow down due to water resistance
It stays the same because the swimmer starts at rest

questions.stem: A wrecking ball swings toward a building wall. Just before impact, a crane exerts a forward horizontal force on the ball in the same direction as the ball's forward displacement. What happens to the ball's ability during this phase?
options: It decreases because the ball is about to hit the wall
It stays the same because gravity is pulling the ball down
It increases because the applied force is in the same direction as the displacement
It increases only if no other forces act on the ball

questions.stem: A person catches a baseball. The catcher's glove exerts a backward force on the ball as the ball moves forward. What happens to the ability stored in the ball's motion?
options: It increases because the catcher is adding force to the system
It stays the same because the ball eventually stops
It decreases because the force is opposite to the displacement
It stays the same because the ball's mass does not change

questions.stem: A car brakes to a stop. The brakes exert a backward friction force on the wheels as the car moves forward. What happens to the ability of the car's motion?
options: It increases because the brakes are engaging
It decreases because the braking force is opposite to the displacement
It stays the same because the car's mass is constant
It increases because the car is decelerating

questions.stem: A sky diver opens a parachute. Air resistance now exerts an upward force on the sky diver as the sky diver continues to fall downward. What happens to the sky diver's ability?
options: It increases because the sky diver is still moving
It stays the same because the parachute balances gravity
It decreases because the air resistance force is opposite to the displacement
It decreases only after the sky diver reaches terminal velocity

questions.stem: A child slides down a snowy hill. Friction exerts a force up the hill on the child as the child slides down the hill. What does friction do to the ability of the child's motion?
options: Friction increases the ability because it slows the child safely
Friction decreases the ability because the frictional force is opposite to the displacement
Friction keeps the ability constant because the slope is steady
Friction increases the ability because the child is gaining speed

questions.stem: A baseball pitcher throws a ball forward, but then air resistance acts backward on the ball as it travels forward toward the plate. What does air resistance do to the ball's ability?
options: It increases the ability because it guides the ball
It has no effect because the ball is already in motion
It decreases the ability because the drag force is opposite to the displacement
It decreases the ability only after the ball passes the halfway point

questions.stem: A sled moves forward across the snow. A child applies a backward-directed brake force on the sled while it moves forward. Which pattern best explains what happens to the ability of the sled?
options: Pattern 1 — force and displacement are in the same direction
Pattern 3 — the force is perpendicular to the displacement
Pattern 2 — the force is opposite to the displacement, decreasing ability
Pattern 4 — a stronger braking force increases ability

questions.stem: A basketball player jumps upward, and after reaching peak height begins to fall. Gravity pulls the player downward while the player moves downward. What happens to the player's gravitational potential ability during the fall?
options: It increases because the player is falling faster
It stays the same because gravity is constant
It decreases because gravity is in the same direction as the displacement
It decreases because the player is losing height

questions.stem: A person pushes a swing backward while the swing moves backward — but then the swing starts moving forward and the person still pushes backward. Now the force is backward and the displacement is forward. What happens to the ability of the swing?
options: It increases because the person is still pushing
It stays the same because the push hasn't changed direction
It decreases because the force is now opposite to the displacement
It increases because the swing is moving faster

questions.stem: A hockey puck slides forward on ice. A goalkeeper's stick pushes backward on the puck as the puck moves forward. What happens to the puck's ability?
options: It increases because the stick is in contact with the puck
It decreases because the stick force is in the opposite direction to the displacement
It stays the same because the ice is frictionless
It increases only if the stick accelerates the puck

questions.stem: A roller coaster car descends a hill. A magnetic braking system exerts an upward force along the track (opposing motion) as the car moves downward along the track. What happens to the car's ability?
options: It increases because the car is going downhill
It decreases because the braking force is opposite to the displacement
It stays the same because the track guides the motion
It increases because gravity still acts on the car

questions.stem: A person lowers a heavy box slowly to the floor. The person exerts an upward force on the box as the box moves downward. What happens to the box's gravitational potential ability?
options: It increases because the person is exerting a force on the box
It stays the same because the box moves slowly
It decreases because the applied force is opposite to the displacement
It increases because the person is holding the box up

questions.stem: A ball is thrown upward. Gravity exerts a downward force on the ball as the ball moves upward. What happens to the ball's ability during the upward phase?
options: It increases because the ball is gaining height
It increases because the ball is still moving upward
It decreases because the gravitational force is opposite to the displacement
It stays the same because gravity is constant

questions.stem: A bicycle rider coasts down a hill and applies the hand brakes. The braking force acts opposite to the direction of motion. What does this tell you about the ability of the bicycle system?
options: The ability increases because the rider is descending
The ability stays the same because the brakes are balanced by gravity
The ability decreases because the braking force is opposite to the displacement
The ability decreases only if the bike comes to a complete stop

questions.stem: An elevator descends from the 10th floor to the ground floor. The cable exerts an upward tension force on the elevator as the elevator moves downward. What does the cable do to the gravitational potential ability of the elevator-Earth system?
options: It increases the potential ability because the cable is pulling up
It decreases the potential ability because the cable force is opposite to the displacement
It has no effect because the cable is just maintaining tension
It increases the potential ability only if the elevator slows down

questions.stem: A spring-loaded toy car is rolling forward. A rubber bumper at the end of a track pushes backward on the car as the car moves forward. What happens to the car's ability?
options: It increases because the bumper stores the ability
It stays the same because the bumper just redirects the car
It decreases because the bumper force is in the opposite direction to the car's displacement
It decreases only when the car is fully stopped

questions.stem: A person presses their hand against a solid brick wall with full force, but the wall does not move. What happens to the ability of the wall system?
options: It increases because the person is exerting a large force
It decreases because the person is pushing very hard
It stays the same because there is no displacement
It increases because the person is tired after pushing

questions.stem: A student carries a stack of books horizontally across a flat floor at constant height. The student exerts an upward force on the books, but the books move horizontally. What happens to the gravitational potential ability of the books?
options: It increases because the student is holding the books up
It increases because the student is walking
It stays the same because the upward force is perpendicular to the horizontal displacement
It decreases because the student is expending effort

questions.stem: A satellite orbits Earth in a perfectly circular orbit. Earth's gravity always points toward the center of the orbit, which is perpendicular to the satellite's velocity and displacement. What happens to the satellite's ability?
options: It increases because gravity is always acting on the satellite
It decreases because the satellite is losing altitude over time
It stays the same because the gravitational force is perpendicular to the displacement
It increases because the satellite moves very fast

questions.stem: A person attempts to push a car that is stuck in mud. They push as hard as they can, but the car does not budge. Which pattern explains what happens to the car's ability?
options: Pattern 1 — force and displacement are the same, increasing ability
Pattern 2 — the force is opposite to the displacement
Pattern 3 — there is no displacement, so the ability does not change
Pattern 4 — a stronger force increases ability more

questions.stem: A nail is hammered into a wall and is now fully seated. A person continues pressing the hammer against the nail head, but the nail no longer moves. What happens to the system's ability?
options: It increases because the hammer is still pressing
It decreases because the person is pushing backward
It stays the same because there is no displacement
It increases because the nail is now under pressure

questions.stem: A horse pulls a plow sideways along a field, but the plow is also sinking vertically into the soil. The horse's pulling force is entirely horizontal. What does the horse's pulling force do to the gravitational potential ability of the plow-Earth system?
options: It increases the potential ability because the horse is doing a lot of pulling
It decreases the potential ability because the force is large
It has no effect on the gravitational potential ability because the horizontal force is perpendicular to the vertical displacement
It increases the potential ability because the plow is moving through the soil

questions.stem: A figure skater glides in a circle on ice. The ice exerts a normal force upward on the skater while the skater moves horizontally. What does the normal force from the ice do to the skater's ability?
options: It increases the ability because the ice supports the skater
It decreases the ability because the skater is on a circular path
It has no effect because the normal force is perpendicular to the displacement
It increases the ability because the skater is moving fast

questions.stem: A book rests on a table. The table exerts an upward normal force on the book, but the book does not move. What happens to the book's ability?
options: It increases because the table is pushing up on the book
It stays the same because there is no displacement
It decreases because gravity is also acting on the book
It stays the same because the two forces cancel out

questions.stem: A bucket of water is swung in a horizontal circle at the end of a rope at constant speed. The rope tension points inward (toward the center), while the bucket moves along the circle (perpendicular to the rope). What happens to the bucket's ability?
options: It increases because the rope is pulling on the bucket
It decreases because the bucket is being restrained
It stays the same because the tension is perpendicular to the displacement
It increases because the bucket is moving in a circle

questions.stem: A construction worker pushes on a structural beam to test if it will move. The beam is bolted to the floor and does not shift at all. What correctly describes the situation?
options: The ability of the beam increases because the worker exerts a large force
The ability of the beam decreases because the worker pushes hard
The ability of the beam is unchanged because there is no displacement
The ability of the beam increases because the worker applies force for a long time

questions.stem: A rock climber pulls down on a rope that is anchored above, and the rope pulls the climber upward as the climber ascends. The force on the climber is upward and the displacement is upward. What happens to the climber's gravitational potential ability?
options: It decreases because climbing is tiring
It stays the same because the rope is just redirecting force
It increases because the force on the climber is in the same direction as the displacement
It increases only after the climber reaches the top

questions.stem: A soccer ball rolls forward across a field. A goalkeeper dives and pushes the ball backward while the ball is still moving forward. Which pattern describes what happens to the ball's ability?
options: Pattern 1 — force and displacement are in the same direction
Pattern 3 — force is perpendicular to displacement
Pattern 2 — force is opposite to displacement, decreasing the ball's ability
Pattern 5 — more displacement means more ability

questions.stem: A student tries to lift a very heavy crate off the floor but cannot move it. Which pattern best explains what happens to the crate's gravitational potential ability?
options: Pattern 1 — the upward force increases the potential ability
Pattern 3 — no displacement means no change in ability
Pattern 2 — the force is opposite to what gravity wants, so ability decreases
Pattern 4 — a stronger force causes more change in ability

questions.stem: A bobsled is pushed by teammates at the start of a run. The pushers exert a forward force on the sled as it moves forward. Then the sled enters the icy track and the pushers let go. During the push phase, what happens to the sled's ability?
options: It decreases because the pushers are behind the sled
It stays the same because the sled is just starting to move
It increases because the push force is in the same direction as the displacement
It increases only after the pushers release the sled

questions.stem: A treadmill belt moves backward beneath a person's feet. The person's feet push backward against the belt, but the person does not move forward — they stay in place. The treadmill belt pushes forward on the person's feet. What happens to the person's ability (they remain stationary)?
options: It increases because the belt pushes the person forward
It stays the same because the person has zero displacement
It decreases because the person is working against the belt
It increases because the person is exerting a large force

questions.stem: A person lowers a barbell slowly from overhead back down to shoulder height. The person exerts an upward force on the barbell while the barbell moves downward. Which pattern best describes what happens to the gravitational potential ability of the barbell-Earth system?
options: Pattern 1 — force and displacement are the same, ability increases
Pattern 3 — no displacement, so no change
Pattern 2 — force is opposite to displacement, potential ability decreases
Pattern 5 — more displacement means more change in ability

questions.stem: A swimmer underwater pushes off the wall horizontally and glides forward. Then a swim coach reaches in and pushes the swimmer's feet upward while the swimmer moves horizontally forward. What does the coach's upward force do to the swimmer's horizontal ability of motion?
options: It increases the swimmer's forward ability because any force helps
It has no effect on the swimmer's forward ability because the upward force is perpendicular to the forward displacement
It decreases the swimmer's forward ability because the force is not aligned with the motion
It increases the swimmer's ability only if the swimmer also moves upward

questions.stem: In a tug of war, Team A pulls the rope to the right with great force, but Team B pulls with equal force to the left and the rope does not move. What happens to the ability of the rope system?
options: It increases because both teams are pulling hard
It stays the same because there is no displacement
It increases on the right side and decreases on the left side
It decreases because the forces cancel out

questions.stem: A crane operator first raises a load upward (force up, displacement up), then swings the load horizontally to a new location at the same height (force up from cable, displacement horizontal). What happens to the gravitational potential ability during the horizontal swing phase?
options: It continues to increase because the crane is still holding the load
It stays the same because the upward cable force is perpendicular to the horizontal displacement
It decreases because the load is moving sideways instead of up
It increases because the crane is expending effort to swing the load

questions.stem: A log is dragged up a ramp by a rope (force up the ramp, displacement up the ramp — ability increases). Then the same log is placed on a frictionless floor and slid sideways by a horizontal push (force horizontal, displacement horizontal — ability increases). In which scenario did the system's ability increase for the same reason?
options: Only the ramp scenario — gravity makes ramps special
Only the sliding scenario — horizontal surfaces are frictionless
Both scenarios — in each case the applied force is in the same direction as the displacement
Neither scenario — ability can only increase when an object moves upward

questions.stem: A student pushes a cart 5 meters across a floor. In Scenario A, she pushes with 10 N of force. In Scenario B, she pushes with 20 N of force. Both forces are in the direction of motion. Which scenario gives the cart's system more ability?
options: Scenario A, because the cart is lighter
Scenario B, because the force is stronger
They gain the same ability because the displacement is the same
Scenario A, because slower pushes store more ability

questions.stem: Two identical springs are compressed the same distance. Spring A is compressed by a 15 N force and Spring B is compressed by a 30 N force. Which spring stores more potential ability?
options: Spring A, because it was compressed first
They store the same potential ability since the compression distance is equal
Spring B, because the force acting on it is stronger
Spring A, because lighter forces are more efficient

questions.stem: A weightlifter raises a barbell 1 meter off the ground. In Lift 1, she exerts 400 N upward. In Lift 2, she exerts 600 N upward. Both lifts move the barbell the same 1 meter. Which lift gives the barbell's system more potential ability?
options: Lift 1, because a lighter lift is more controlled
Lift 2, because the upward force is stronger
Both lifts give the same potential ability
Lift 1, because the barbell weighs the same in both cases

questions.stem: Two archers each draw their bowstring back the same distance. Archer A pulls with 50 N of force and Archer B pulls with 80 N of force. Whose bow stores more potential ability?
options: Archer A's bow, because less force means the string is under less stress
They store equal potential ability because the draw distance is the same
Archer B's bow, because the pulling force is stronger
It depends on the weight of the arrow

questions.stem: A sled is pulled 4 meters along a snowy path. In Trial 1, the pulling force is 25 N in the direction of motion. In Trial 2, the pulling force is 40 N in the direction of motion. Which trial gives the sled's system more ability?
options: Trial 1, because the sled is moving slower
Trial 2, because the force is greater
Both trials give equal ability since displacement is the same
Trial 1, because a smaller force is applied over the same distance

questions.stem: A catapult arm is pushed down 0.5 meters by two different crews. Crew A pushes with 200 N and Crew B pushes with 350 N. Which catapult stores more potential ability in its mechanism?
options: Crew A's catapult, because less force was used
They store the same potential ability since displacement is identical
Crew B's catapult, because the applied force is stronger
It cannot be determined without knowing the catapult's mass

questions.stem: In a lab, two rubber bands are stretched the same 10 cm. Band A is stretched with 5 N and Band B is stretched with 12 N. Which rubber band has more potential ability stored in it?
options: Band A, because it was stretched with less force and may be less damaged
Band B, because the stretching force was stronger
They have equal potential ability since the stretch distance is the same
Band A, because thinner rubber bands store more ability

questions.stem: A furniture mover pushes a heavy dresser 3 meters across a room. On Monday she pushes with 100 N. On Tuesday she pushes the same dresser the same 3 meters but with 150 N. On which day does the dresser's system gain more ability?
options: Monday, because the dresser is the same and the distance is the same
Tuesday, because the applied force is greater
Both days give the same ability gain
Monday, because a slower push stores more ability

questions.stem: A rocket engine fires for exactly 2 seconds, pushing a rocket forward. In Test 1 the thrust is 5000 N. In Test 2 the thrust is 8000 N. If both rockets travel the same distance during those 2 seconds, which rocket's system gains more ability?
options: Test 1, because a lower thrust is safer
Test 2, because the thrust force is larger
Both rockets gain equal ability if they travel the same distance
Test 1, because less fuel is burned

questions.stem: Two identical boxes are slid along the floor the same 6 meters. Box A is pushed with 30 N in the direction of motion. Box B is pushed with 30 N at an angle perpendicular to the motion, plus an additional 20 N in the direction of motion. Which box's system gains more ability from the forces acting in the direction of motion?
options: Box A, because only one force acts on it
Box B, because the total force on it is larger and part of it is in the direction of motion
They gain the same ability because the displacement is the same
Box A, because perpendicular forces cancel out ability gains

questions.stem: A student kicks a soccer ball. Kick A delivers 60 N of force over the 0.2 m the foot is in contact with the ball. Kick B delivers 90 N over the same 0.2 m contact distance. Which kick gives the ball more ability?
options: Kick A, because less force reduces wear on the ball
Both kicks give equal ability since the contact distance is the same
Kick B, because the force applied is stronger
Kick A, because a lighter kick maintains more control

questions.stem: A crane lifts a steel beam 8 meters. Crane A uses 2000 N of upward force. Crane B uses 3500 N of upward force. The beam travels the same 8 meters in both cases. Which crane gives the beam's system more potential ability?
options: Crane A, because it uses less force
Crane B, because the lifting force is greater
Both cranes give the beam equal potential ability
It depends on the speed of the lift

questions.stem: Two students push shopping carts 10 meters down an aisle. Student A pushes with 20 N. Student B pushes with 45 N. Both push in the direction of motion. Which statement is correct?
options: Student A's cart gains more ability because lighter force is more efficient over distance
Student B's cart gains more ability because a stronger force was applied over the same displacement
Both carts gain the same ability since they travel the same 10 meters
Neither cart gains ability because the floor is flat

questions.stem: A hydraulic press compresses a metal cube the same 2 cm in two tests. Test X applies 500 N and Test Y applies 1200 N, both directed into the cube. In which test does the cube's system gain more ability?
options: Test X, because lower force causes less damage to the cube
Test Y, because the compressing force is stronger
Both tests give the cube equal ability since the compression distance is the same
Test X, because the cube resists more at lower forces

questions.stem: A child pulls a toy wagon 5 meters forward. On the first pull she uses 8 N. On the second pull she uses 8 N but adds a 4 N bungee cord also pulling forward. Both pulls cover the same 5 meters. In which pull does the wagon's system gain more ability?
options: First pull, because only one force acts on the wagon
Second pull, because the total forward force is greater
Both pulls give the wagon equal ability since the distance is the same
Second pull only if the bungee cord is attached at the front

questions.stem: A student pushes a box with a constant 15 N force in the direction of motion. In Case 1, the box moves 2 meters. In Case 2, the box moves 5 meters. Which case gives the box's system more ability?
options: Case 1, because the box stops sooner and stores ability
Case 2, because the displacement is greater
Both cases give the same ability since the force is identical
Case 1, because shorter distances preserve more ability

questions.stem: A weightlifter uses a constant upward force of 500 N to raise a barbell. In Lift A she raises it 0.5 m. In Lift B she raises it 1.5 m. Which lift gives the barbell's system more potential ability?
options: Lift A, because less displacement means more controlled storage
Both lifts give the same potential ability since the force is constant
Lift B, because the displacement is greater
Lift A, because the barbell is heavier closer to the ground

questions.stem: An archer pulls a bowstring with a constant 60 N force. In Draw 1, she pulls the string back 15 cm. In Draw 2, she pulls it back 30 cm. Which draw stores more potential ability in the bow?
options: Draw 1, because a shorter draw is more accurate
Draw 2, because the string displacement is greater
Both draws store the same potential ability since the force is equal
Draw 1, because the string is under less strain

questions.stem: A sled is pulled with a constant 40 N force along flat snow. Trial 1 covers 3 meters. Trial 2 covers 7 meters. In which trial does the sled's system gain more ability?
options: Trial 1, because less distance means more concentrated ability
Both trials give equal ability since the force is the same
Trial 2, because the sled travels a greater distance
Trial 1, because friction removes ability over longer distances

questions.stem: A rubber band is stretched with a constant 10 N force. In Stretch A it is pulled 5 cm. In Stretch B it is pulled 12 cm. Which rubber band has more potential ability stored in it?
options: Stretch A, because the band is less likely to break
Both stretches store equal potential ability since the force is constant
Stretch B, because the displacement is greater
Stretch A, because shorter stretches are more efficient

questions.stem: A rocket engine produces a constant 10,000 N of thrust. In Flight 1 the rocket travels 100 m before the engine cuts out. In Flight 2 the rocket travels 400 m before the engine cuts out. Which flight gives the rocket's system more ability?
options: Flight 1, because a shorter burn is more efficient
Flight 2, because the rocket is displaced a greater distance while the force acts
Both flights give the same ability since the thrust force is equal
Flight 1, because the rocket moves faster at shorter distances

questions.stem: A furniture mover pushes a couch with a steady 80 N force. On Monday she moves it 4 meters. On Friday she moves the same couch with the same 80 N but covers 9 meters. On which day does the couch's system gain more ability?
options: Monday, because shorter distances are less tiring
Friday, because the couch is displaced a greater distance
Both days give the couch equal ability since the applied force is the same
Monday, because the couch weighs the same on both days

questions.stem: A catapult arm is pushed down with a constant 300 N force. In Setup 1, the arm is pushed down 0.3 m. In Setup 2, the arm is pushed down 0.9 m. Which setup stores more potential ability?
options: Setup 1, because less compression means faster release
Setup 2, because the arm is displaced a greater distance
Both setups store equal potential ability since the force is the same
Setup 1, because potential ability decreases with greater compression

questions.stem: A crane lifts a steel beam with a constant 4000 N upward force. In Job 1 the beam is raised 3 meters. In Job 2 the beam is raised 10 meters. Which job gives the beam's system more potential ability?
options: Job 1, because a lower height stores ability more safely
Both jobs give the same potential ability since the crane force is equal
Job 2, because the displacement of the beam is greater
Job 1, because gravity removes potential ability at greater heights

questions.stem: A student compresses a spring with a constant 25 N force. In Trial 1 she compresses it 2 cm. In Trial 2 she compresses it 8 cm. Which trial stores more potential ability in the spring?
options: Trial 1, because less compression preserves the spring's structure
Trial 2, because the spring is compressed a greater distance
Both trials store equal potential ability since the force is the same
Trial 1, because springs lose ability when over-compressed

questions.stem: Two identical balls are each kicked with the same 70 N force. Ball A is in contact with the foot for 0.1 m. Ball B is in contact with the foot for 0.3 m. Which ball receives more ability from the kick?
options: Ball A, because shorter contact time means a sharper kick
Both balls receive equal ability since the kicking force is the same
Ball B, because the foot displaces the ball over a greater distance
Ball A, because the force is applied more quickly

questions.stem: A student drags a heavy box with a constant 50 N horizontal force. She drags it 2 meters in the morning and 6 meters in the afternoon. In which session does the box's system gain more ability?
options: Morning session, because the box is dragged when it is freshest
Afternoon session, because the displacement is greater
Both sessions give equal ability since the pulling force is the same
Morning session, because shorter drags are more efficient

questions.stem: A bow-and-arrow system is drawn with a constant 55 N force. Archer 1 draws the string back 20 cm. Archer 2 draws the string back 35 cm. Rank the potential ability stored from least to greatest.

Archer 1, then Archer 2 — longer draw distances reduce stored potential ability

questions.stem: A constant 120 N force pushes a cart forward. The cart moves 1 m in Scenario X and 5 m in Scenario Y. Which statement correctly compares the ability gained?

Scenario Y gives more ability because the greater displacement means more ability is added to the system

Spring Q, because the same force is applied over a greater displacement, adding more potential ability

questions.stem: Scenario A: A 20 N force pushes a box 4 meters. Scenario B: A 40 N force pushes a box 2 meters. Both forces are in the direction of motion. Which scenario gives the box's system more ability?
options: Scenario A, because the displacement is greater
Scenario B, because the force is stronger

questions.stem: A weightlifter performs two lifts. Lift 1: 300 N upward over 2 meters. Lift 2: 200 N upward over 4 meters. Which lift gives the barbell's system more potential ability?

Lift 1, because heavier lifts always store more potential ability

questions.stem: Sled A is pulled with 50 N over 6 meters. Sled B is pulled with 75 N over 3 meters. Both forces are in the direction of motion. Which sled's system gains more ability?

Sled B, because the pulling force is stronger
Sled A, because 50×6 = 300, which is greater than 75×3 = 225

questions.stem: A spring is compressed in two trials. Trial 1: 100 N over 5 cm. Trial 2: 250 N over 2 cm. Which trial stores more potential ability in the spring?

Trial 1, because 100×5 = 500, which is greater than 250×2 = 500 — they are equal
Trial 1, because greater displacement always stores more potential ability
Trial 2, because stronger forces always compress springs more efficiently

Test 1, because 6000×50 = 300,000 which is greater than 3000×80 = 240,000

Bow Y, because 40×60 = 2400, while 80×25 = 2000, so Bow Y stores more potential ability
Bow X, because 80×25 = 2000, while 40×60 = 2400 — wait, Bow Y wins

Setup Q, because 200×1.0 = 200, while 400×0.4 = 160, so Setup Q stores more

Scenario 1, because 60×5 = 300, which is greater than 90×3 = 270
Scenario 2, because 90×3 = 270, which is greater than 60×5 = 300 — wait, Scenario 1 wins

Crane 2, because 2500×10 = 25,000, which is greater than 5000×4 = 20,000

Box A, because 35×8 = 280, which is greater than 70×3 = 210
Box B, because 70×3 = 210, which is greater than 35×8 = 280 — so Box A wins

questions.stem: A student holds a heavy book perfectly still at arm's length for 5 minutes. How does the book's system's potential ability change during those 5 minutes?

It stays the same, because the book does not move and there is no displacement

The car's ability decreases because a sideways force slows the system
The car's ability increases slightly because any force adds some ability

questions.stem: Rank these three scenarios from LEAST to MOST ability given to the cart's system. All forces are in the direction of motion. A: 10 N over 3 m. B: 30 N over 1 m. C: 5 N over 5 m.

B (30), then C (25), then A (30) — A and B tie
C (25), then A (30) and B (30) tied — A and B both greater than C

The sled gains no additional ability in the second half because displacement is zero after hitting the wall
The sled gains less ability in the second half because the wall resists the force
The sled gains equal ability in both halves because force and displacement balance out

Student 1's cart, because a lighter cart gains ability more efficiently

The bicycle's ability stays the same because the force is perpendicular to the wheel spin
The bicycle's ability decreases because the force is opposite to the displacement

The ball's ability decreased
The ball's ability stayed the same

The ball's ability decreases

The stored potential ability decreases

The car's ability decreases because the person is exerting effort

The javelin's ability stays the same
The javelin gains potential ability from the air

The sled's ability stays the same because the dog is an animal, not a machine
The sled's ability increases because force and displacement point in the same direction

questions.stem: A person opens a door by pulling the handle directly toward themselves. The handle moves toward the person (same direction as the force). What happens to the door's stored ability?

The door's ability decreases

questions.stem: A magnet attracts a steel ball toward it. The magnetic force pulls the ball in the same direction the ball moves. What happens to the ball's ability?

The heavier puck always gains more ability

Scenario Y's gate, because more force was applied per meter
Both gates gain equal potential ability because the force is the same

questions.stem: A river pushes a rock downstream with 100 N over 3 m. Then a dam wall pushes the same rock UPSTREAM (opposite to motion) with 60 N over 1 m. Overall, what happened to the rock's ability?

The rock's ability decreased overall, because the dam's force was stronger per meter
The rock's ability stayed the same, because the two forces are in opposite directions

questions.stem: Operative Nyx applies a 25 N force PERPENDICULAR to a vault door's displacement. Operative Rex applies a 10 N force in the SAME direction as the vault door's displacement over the same distance. Which operative increases the vault's stored ability more?

Operative Rex, because only a force in the same direction as displacement increases stored ability — Operative Nyx's perpendicular force contributes nothing

questions.stem: A spring is compressed 0.2 m by a 60 N force directed the same way as the compression displacement. The potential ability stored in the spring decreases because pushing sideways relieves tension
The potential ability stored in the spring stays the same because the sideways force is perpendicular to any further compression

Operative B, because greater force over the same displacement increases stored ability more

questions.stem: Scenario A: A gust of wind pushes a kite with 50 N in the same direction as the kite's 6 m displacement. questions.stem: Operative Kade pushes a vault door with 60 N in the same direction as the 3 m displacement, then a counterweight pulls the door back with 20 N opposite to the door's next 1 m of displacement. Operative Silva pushes a different vault with 60 N perpendicular to its 3 m displacement. Which vault system ends up with MORE total stored ability added to it?
options: Operative Kade's vault, because same-direction force over 3 m adds ability, while the counter-force over 1 m removes less; Operative Silva's vault gains nothing from the perpendicular push
Operative Silva's vault, because no ability is ever removed from it
Both vaults gain the same stored ability
Operative Kade's vault gains nothing because the counterweight cancels everything

questions.stem: A student claims: 'If two forces act on a system and one increases its stored ability while the other decreases it, the net change is always zero.' Is this claim correct?
options: Yes — opposing forces always cancel completely
No — the net change depends on the magnitude of each force AND the displacement over which each acts; they only cancel if both products are equal
Yes — but only if the forces are equal in magnitude
No — perpendicular forces always dominate

questions.stem: Runner A is pushed forward by wind with 20 N over 100 m, but friction acts backward with 5 N over the same 100 m. Runner B experiences the same wind (20 N forward) over 50 m and the same friction (5 N backward) over 50 m. Which runner experiences a greater NET increase in stored ability?
options: Runner A, because the net force acts over a greater displacement
Runner B, because shorter distances mean less friction loss
Both runners experience the same net increase because the ratio of forces is equal
Runner B, because friction has more time to act on Runner A

questions.stem: Which of the following combinations would produce the GREATEST increase in a system's stored ability?
options: A 10 N force perpendicular to displacement over 100 m
A 500 N force opposite to displacement over 0.01 m
A 50 N force in the same direction as displacement over 10 m
A 1000 N force with zero displacement

questions.stem: A crane lifts a steel beam 8 m upward using a force of 600 N. A second crane lifts an identical beam 8 m upward using a force of 900 N. Which statement best compares the potential ability gained by each beam system?
options: Both systems gain the same potential ability because the displacement is the same.
The second system gains more potential ability because the force is stronger over the same displacement.
The first system gains more potential ability because it is more efficient.
Neither system gains potential ability because cranes are machines, not living things.

questions.stem: An astronaut in deep space pushes a satellite with 200 N in the same direction it is drifting for 3 m. Then the astronaut pushes a second identical satellite with 200 N perpendicular to its motion for 3 m. How does the ability gained by each satellite system compare?
options: Both satellite systems gain the same ability because the force and displacement magnitudes are equal.
The first satellite system gains more ability because force in the same direction as displacement increases stored ability.
The second satellite system gains more ability because perpendicular forces are more effective.
The first satellite gains less ability because opposing its natural motion wastes force.

questions.stem: A diver underwater pushes a large crate along the seafloor with 150 N for 5 m. Another diver pushes an identical crate with 150 N for 10 m. Which of the following correctly describes the difference in ability gained?
options: Both crate systems gain the same ability because the force is the same.
The first crate system gains more ability because shorter distance means less resistance.
The second crate system gains more ability because greater displacement with the same force increases stored ability.
Neither crate system gains ability because water friction cancels the effect.

questions.stem: A spring-loaded toy launcher compresses a spring 0.04 m using a force of 80 N. A second launcher compresses a spring the same 0.04 m using a force of 120 N. A student says both springs store the same potential ability because the compression distance is equal. Is the student correct?
options: Yes — displacement alone determines how much potential ability is stored.
No — the second spring stores more potential ability because a stronger force over the same displacement increases stored potential ability.
Yes — the spring material determines stored potential ability, not the force applied.
No — the first spring stores more potential ability because less force is more efficient.

questions.stem: A bulldozer pushes a pile of rubble 12 m with a force of 4000 N in the direction of motion. At the same time, wind blows sideways against the rubble with a force of 500 N. How do the bulldozer and wind each affect the stored ability of the rubble system?
options: Both increase stored ability equally because both exert force while the rubble moves.
The bulldozer increases stored ability; the wind has no effect because its force is perpendicular to the displacement.
The wind increases stored ability more because sideways forces act over a longer path.
Both fail to change stored ability because the rubble is not part of either system.

questions.stem: A guitarist pulls a string 0.01 m from rest with a force of 5 N, storing potential ability in the string. If the guitarist instead pulls the same string 0.02 m with the same 5 N force, and a piano key is pressed down 0.008 m with a force of 5 N, which system stores the most potential ability?
options: The piano key system, because pressing keys is more direct than pulling strings.
The first guitar string system, because the original technique is more familiar.
The second guitar string system, because the same force over greater displacement stores more potential ability.
All three systems store the same potential ability because the force is equal.

questions.stem: Two construction workers push identical boxes across a floor. Worker A pushes with 300 N in the direction of motion for 6 m. Worker B pushes with 300 N at a 90° angle to the direction of motion for 6 m. A third worker pushes with 300 N opposite to the direction of motion for 6 m. How does the stored ability change for each box system?
options: Worker A's box gains ability; Worker B's box is unchanged; Worker C's box loses ability.
All three boxes gain the same ability because the force magnitudes and distances are all equal.
Worker A's box gains ability; Workers B and C both cause a loss of ability.
Worker B's box gains the most ability because perpendicular forces are the most powerful.

questions.stem: An astronaut applies a force of 50 N to a space probe for 2 m in the same direction it is moving, then immediately applies a force of 50 N opposite to the probe's motion for 2 m. What is the net change in the probe system's stored ability after both pushes?
options: The system has a net gain in stored ability because the astronaut applied force twice.
The system returns to its original stored ability because the increase from the first push is canceled by the decrease from the second push.
The system loses stored ability overall because opposing forces are always dominant.
The system gains stored ability equal to twice the original push because motion compounds effects.

questions.stem: A rubber band car is launched by stretching a rubber band 0.06 m with a force of 10 N. The same rubber band is then stretched 0.06 m with a force of 25 N. Finally, a different rubber band is stretched 0.12 m with a force of 10 N. Rank the stored potential ability from greatest to least.
options: Second setup > Third setup > First setup
Third setup > Second setup > First setup
First setup = Second setup = Third setup, because rubber bands all store the same amount
Second setup > First setup, and Third setup > First setup — but Second and Third cannot be compared without more information

questions.stem: A crane lifts a beam 5 m upward with 800 N of force. A second crane tries to lift an identical beam but it doesn't move — the cable is stuck — and the crane exerts 800 N for 30 seconds. Compare the potential ability gained by each beam system.
options: Both beams gain the same potential ability because the same force was applied.
The second beam gains more potential ability because more time means more accumulation.
Only the first beam system gains potential ability; the second beam system gains none because there is no displacement.
The second beam gains less but still some potential ability because of the sustained force.

questions.stem: A diver pushes a sealed barrel horizontally along the seafloor with 200 N for 4 m. The ocean current simultaneously pushes the barrel horizontally with 80 N in the same direction for the same 4 m. How does the total stored ability change in the barrel system?
options: Only the diver increases the stored ability; currents cannot affect sealed objects.
Both the diver and the current increase the stored ability of the barrel system, and the combined effect is greater than either alone.
The current cancels the diver's effect because they are both horizontal.
The barrel system's stored ability does not change because underwater forces are balanced.

questions.stem: A piano key is pressed down 0.005 m with 30 N of force. A guitar string is pulled 0.005 m with 30 N of force perpendicular to the string's length. A drumstick strikes a drumhead moving it 0.005 m downward with 30 N directly downward. Which instrument system stores the most potential ability from the musician's action?
options: The guitar string stores the most because strings are designed to store potential ability.
All three store equal potential ability because the force and displacement magnitudes are the same.
The piano and drum systems store equal potential ability; the guitar system stores none because the force is perpendicular to string motion.
The drum system stores the least because it is struck, not pushed steadily.

questions.stem: Two satellites drift past an astronaut. Satellite A is moving left at 2 m/s, and the astronaut pushes it to the right with 100 N for 3 m. Satellite B is moving right at 2 m/s, and the astronaut pushes it to the right with 100 N for 3 m. How does the stored ability change for each satellite system?
options: Both satellite systems gain the same stored ability because the force and displacement are equal.
Satellite A's system loses stored ability; Satellite B's system gains stored ability.
Satellite B's system gains more stored ability than Satellite A's system because they move in the same direction.
Neither system changes stored ability because the satellites were already moving.

questions.stem: A construction crew uses two cranes to lift a single steel plate. Crane 1 exerts 1200 N upward and Crane 2 exerts 800 N upward. Together they lift the plate 10 m. How much potential ability is added to the plate system, compared to if only Crane 1 had lifted it the same 10 m?
options: The same — only the strongest force matters when two forces act together.
More — a stronger combined force over the same displacement increases stored potential ability more than a weaker force alone.
Less — sharing the lift between two cranes reduces each crane's effectiveness.
The same — displacement determines stored potential ability, not the force applied.

questions.stem: A rubber band is pulled back 0.03 m with 15 N of force, then released. As it snaps forward 0.03 m back to its natural length, what happens to the stored potential ability of the rubber band system?
options: It increases because the rubber band is moving.
It stays the same because the rubber band returns to where it started.
It decreases because the rubber band's restoring force acts opposite to the direction of its displacement as it returns.
It increases then decreases symmetrically, so there is no net change.

questions.stem: Underwater, a diver pushes a buoy downward with 500 N while the buoy moves downward 2 m. Then the buoy's own buoyancy pushes it upward with 400 N as it rises 2 m back to the surface. How do these two interactions affect the stored potential ability of the buoy system?
options: The diver increases stored potential ability; the buoyancy also increases it on the way up.
The diver increases stored potential ability; the buoyancy decreases it on the way back up.
Both interactions leave stored potential ability unchanged because the net displacement is zero.
Buoyancy never changes stored potential ability because it is a natural force, not an external system.

questions.stem: A spring-loaded launcher fires a ball upward. As the spring expands from compressed to natural length (a displacement of 0.05 m), it pushes the ball upward with an average force of 200 N. Gravity simultaneously pulls the ball downward with 2 N over the same 0.05 m. Which force increases the ball system's stored ability more, and why?
options: Gravity increases stored ability more because it always acts on the ball.
The spring increases stored ability more because it exerts a greater force in the same direction as displacement.
Both forces increase stored ability equally because both act over the same displacement.
The spring increases stored ability; gravity decreases it. The net change depends on which is stronger.

questions.stem: A bulldozer pushes a boulder with 5000 N for 8 m across a flat surface. The ground exerts a friction force of 1000 N opposite to the boulder's motion for the same 8 m. What is the combined effect of the bulldozer and friction on the boulder system's stored ability?
options: Stored ability increases by the full effect of the bulldozer's force alone, since friction is irrelevant.
The bulldozer increases stored ability; friction decreases it. The net change depends on the difference between these effects.
Friction cancels the bulldozer, so stored ability stays the same.
Both the bulldozer and friction increase stored ability because both exert force while the boulder moves.

questions.stem: An astronaut pushes two identical space containers. Container A is pushed 6 m with 100 N. Container B is pushed 3 m with 200 N. A student claims Container B gains more stored ability because the force is stronger. Is the student correct?
options: Yes — stronger force always means more stored ability.
No — Container A gains more stored ability because longer displacement always wins.
It cannot be determined — you need to compare force multiplied by displacement for each container.
No — both containers gain the same stored ability because the factors balance out perfectly.

questions.stem: A crane lifts a beam 4 m upward with 700 N. A second crane lowers an identical beam 4 m downward by letting it descend while exerting 700 N upward to control its fall. How do the changes in stored potential ability compare for each beam system?
options: Both beam systems gain potential ability because both cranes apply upward force.
The first beam system gains potential ability; the second beam system loses potential ability, because in the second case the force is opposite to the displacement.
Both beam systems lose potential ability because gravity acts on both beams.
Neither system changes potential ability because the crane forces balance gravity.

questions.stem: A student pushes against a brick wall with all their strength for 10 seconds. The wall does not move. The student claims they increased the wall system's stored ability because they applied a lot of force. What is wrong with this reasoning?
options: Nothing is wrong — strong sustained force always increases a system's stored ability.
The reasoning is wrong because time, not displacement, determines stored ability.
The reasoning is wrong because there is no displacement, so the external system cannot change the stored ability of the wall system.
The reasoning is wrong because the student is not strong enough to qualify as an external system.

questions.stem: A soccer ball rolls across a frictionless floor while gravity pulls it downward. A student claims the ball's stored ability is increasing because it is moving. What is wrong with this reasoning?
options: Nothing — a moving object always has increasing stored ability.
The reasoning is wrong because gravity is perpendicular to the ball's horizontal motion, so gravity cannot change the ball system's stored ability in this case.
The reasoning is wrong because stored ability only applies to objects that are falling.
The reasoning is wrong because frictionless floors cancel all ability changes.

questions.stem: A heavier box and a lighter box are each pushed 3 m with 50 N of force in the direction of motion. A student claims the heavier box gains more stored ability because it weighs more. Is the student correct?
options: Yes — heavier objects always store more ability.
No — stored ability gained depends on the force applied and displacement, not on how heavy the object is.
Yes — more mass means more resistance, which means more ability is stored.
No — the lighter box gains more stored ability because it is easier to move.

questions.stem: An astronaut pushes a satellite with 500 N perpendicular to its motion for 10 m. A classmate says: 'That's a huge force over a big distance — the satellite must have gained a lot of stored ability.' What is wrong with this conclusion?
options: Nothing — large force over large distance always means large stored ability gain.
The force is too large; oversized forces decrease stored ability.
When force is perpendicular to displacement, the external system cannot change the stored ability of the satellite system, regardless of how strong the force is or how far the satellite moves.
The satellite was already moving, so no additional stored ability can be added.

questions.stem: Two students each push a box across the same floor. Student A pushes with 400 N and the box moves 2 m. Student B pushes with 50 N and the box moves 2 m. A third student says 'Student A definitely increased the box system's stored ability way more because 400 N is a much bigger force.' Is this reasoning fully correct?
options: Yes — more force always means more stored ability, no matter what.
Only if both forces are in the same direction as the displacement — assuming they are, then yes, the stronger force increases stored ability more over the same displacement.
No — displacement is the only factor that matters, so both students produced the same stored ability change.
No — Student B's box gains more stored ability because smaller forces are more efficient.

questions.stem: A crane holds a steel beam perfectly still in the air, exerting 2000 N upward for 5 minutes. A student says the beam system gained a huge amount of potential ability because of the enormous sustained force. What is the flaw in this reasoning?
options: The reasoning is correct — sustained strong forces always build up potential ability over time.
The flaw is that the beam would need to be moving upward for potential ability to increase; holding still means no displacement, so potential ability does not change.
The flaw is that cranes are machines and cannot act as external systems.
The flaw is that 5 minutes is too short a time to accumulate potential ability.

questions.stem: A toy car is launched by a rubber band and rolls across the floor while a magnet pulls it sideways with 3 N. The car moves forward 2 m. A student claims the magnet increased the car system's stored ability because it exerted a constant force the entire time. Is the student correct?
options: Yes — any sustained force increases stored ability as long as the object moves.
No — the magnet's force is perpendicular to the car's motion, so it cannot change the car system's stored ability.
Yes — magnetic forces are especially effective at increasing stored ability.
No — only forces in the direction opposite to motion can change stored ability.

questions.stem: A diver pushes a heavy box downward with 300 N, but the box doesn't move because it is wedged between two rocks. The diver pushes for 20 seconds. How does the stored ability of the box system change?
options: It increases steadily over the 20 seconds because the diver applies force the whole time.
It increases once, at the moment the diver first touches the box.
It does not change because there is no displacement, regardless of how long or how hard the diver pushes.
It decreases because the diver is underwater, where forces dissipate.

questions.stem: A guitar string is plucked and vibrates back and forth. During one half of its vibration, the restoring force and displacement are in the same direction. During the other half, the restoring force and displacement are in opposite directions. A student says: 'The string is always moving so its stored potential ability is always increasing.' What is wrong?
options: Nothing — a vibrating object always increases stored potential ability.

The reasoning is wrong: when force and displacement are in the same direction, stored potential ability increases; when they are opposite, stored potential ability decreases. The student is ignoring the direction relationship.
The reasoning is wrong because only external systems can change stored potential ability, and the string's own restoring force is internal.
The reasoning is correct because vibrations are a special case where stored potential ability only increases.

questions.stem: A student pushes a shopping cart with 60 N for 5 m, then pushes it with 120 N for 0 m (the cart hits a wall). The student says: 'I doubled my force, so I must have doubled the stored ability in the second push.' What is wrong with this reasoning?
options: Nothing is wrong — doubling force always doubles stored ability.
The second push produced no displacement, so the stored ability of the cart system did not change during the second push, regardless of the force.
The cart hit a wall, so all stored ability was transferred to the wall instead.
Stored ability can only double if the displacement also doubles.

questions.stem: An astronaut in a spacesuit exerts 80 N on a floating space crate perpendicular to its path of motion. The crate moves 6 m. A second astronaut exerts 0 N on an identical crate, which also moves 6 m (drifting freely). A student says the first astronaut's push gave the first crate more stored ability. Is this correct?
options: Yes — any force greater than zero increases stored ability.
No — both crates experience the same stored ability change because their displacements are equal.
No — the first astronaut's perpendicular push cannot change the first crate's stored ability; both crates experience no change in stored ability from these interactions.
Yes — force always matters more than direction when comparing stored ability changes.

questions.stem: A 5 kg box and a 50 kg box are each lifted 3 m upward by a crane. Each crane exerts exactly the right force to lift its box (no more, no less). A student claims both boxes gain the same potential ability because they move the same distance. Is this correct?
options: Yes — displacement alone determines potential ability gained.
No — the heavier box requires a stronger force to lift the same distance, and a stronger force over the same displacement stores more potential ability.
Yes — weight doesn't affect potential ability because it is an internal property.
No — the lighter box gains more potential ability because less force is needed.

questions.stem: A student tries to compress a stiff spring but the spring doesn't budge — the spring is too stiff. The student exerts 500 N for 10 seconds. What happens to the stored potential ability of the spring system?
options: It increases significantly because 500 N is a large force.
It increases slowly over time as the sustained force gradually compresses the spring.
It does not change because the spring does not move, so there is no displacement.
It decreases because the student's force opposes the spring's natural state.

questions.stem: A piano key is pressed straight down with 20 N for 0.01 m. A guitar string directly next to it is simultaneously vibrated by sound waves that push it sideways with 20 N over 0.01 m of sideways motion. A student says both instruments gain the same stored potential ability. Is this correct?
options: Yes — both involve 20 N over 0.01 m, so the stored ability is identical.
No — the piano key stores more potential ability because downward forces are always stronger.
It depends on which instrument is made of more expensive materials.

Only if the sideways vibration of the string is in the direction of the string's restoring force; if the sound wave pushes perpendicular to the restoring force, the string gains no stored potential ability from that interaction.

questions.stem: A bulldozer pushes a rock pile with 8000 N in the direction of motion for 15 m. A student argues: 'The rock pile is very heavy, so it gained more stored ability than a lighter pile would have.' Is this argument valid?
options: Yes — heavier objects always gain more stored ability from the same push.
No — if the same external force acts over the same displacement in the same direction, the stored ability gained is the same regardless of how heavy the object is.
Yes — more mass means more ability is naturally available to be stored.
No — lighter objects gain more stored ability because they accelerate more.

questions.stem: A satellite orbits Earth in a perfect circle. Earth's gravity pulls the satellite directly toward Earth's center, while the satellite moves along its curved path — always perpendicular to the gravitational pull. Which pattern best explains why Earth's gravity does not change the satellite system's stored ability?
options: Pattern 1 — force in the same direction as displacement increases stored ability.
Pattern 3 — a force perpendicular to displacement cannot change stored ability.
Pattern 4 — stronger forces over equal displacements store more ability.
Pattern 2 — force opposite to displacement decreases stored ability.

questions.stem: A diver exerts a force on a buoy in the same direction the buoy is sinking, causing the buoy to sink faster and deeper. The buoy's stored potential ability increases. Which pattern best explains this outcome?
options: Pattern 3 — an external system fails to change stored ability if force is perpendicular.
Pattern 5 — greater displacement with the same force increases stored ability.
Pattern 1 — an external system increases stored ability by exerting force in the same direction as displacement.
Pattern 2 — force opposite to displacement decreases stored ability.

questions.stem: A student presses a rubber band car's rubber band with equal force but stretches it further than usual. The car launches with noticeably more ability than before. Which pattern best explains why?
options: Pattern 1 — force in the same direction as displacement increases stored ability.
Pattern 4 — stronger force over the same displacement stores more ability.
Pattern 5 — the same force over greater displacement stores more ability.
Pattern 2 — force opposite to displacement decreases stored ability.

questions.stem: A construction crane holds a steel beam at a fixed height for 3 minutes without raising or lowering it. Despite the large upward force being applied, the beam's stored potential ability does not change. Which pattern best explains this?
options: Pattern 2 — force opposite to displacement decreases stored ability.
Pattern 4 — stronger force over the same displacement stores more ability.
Pattern 3 — an external system cannot change stored ability if it cannot displace the object in the system.
Pattern 1 — force in the same direction as displacement increases stored ability.

questions.stem: An astronaut slows down a drifting satellite by pushing it opposite to its motion for 5 m. The satellite's stored ability decreases. Which pattern best explains this?
options: Pattern 1 — force in the same direction as displacement increases stored ability.
Pattern 3 — perpendicular force cannot change stored ability.
Pattern 5 — greater displacement stores more ability.
Pattern 2 — force in the opposite direction of displacement decreases the system's stored ability.

questions.stem: A spring launcher shoots two identical balls. Ball A is launched with the spring compressed 2 cm (a short push) and Ball B is launched with the spring compressed 4 cm (a longer push) using the same average spring force each time. Ball B reaches a higher height. Which pattern best explains why Ball B has more stored ability after launch?
options: Pattern 4 — stronger force over the same displacement stores more ability.
Pattern 5 — the same force over greater displacement stores more ability.
Pattern 3 — perpendicular force cannot change stored ability.
Pattern 2 — force opposite to displacement decreases stored ability.

questions.stem: Two cranes lift identical beams. Crane A lifts with 600 N and Crane B lifts with 900 N — both lifting the same 10 m height. Crane B's beam reaches the same height but with more stored potential ability. Which pattern explains why Crane B's beam system stores more potential ability?
options: Pattern 5 — the same force over greater displacement stores more ability.
Pattern 3 — perpendicular force fails to change stored ability.
Pattern 4 — when displacement is the same, a stronger force stores more ability.
Pattern 2 — force opposite to displacement decreases stored ability.

questions.stem: A bulldozer pushes a boulder while the wind blows sideways against the boulder. The bulldozer increases the boulder system's stored ability, but the wind does not. Which pattern best explains the wind's ineffectiveness?
options: Pattern 2 — the wind's force is opposite to the boulder's displacement, decreasing stored ability.
Pattern 1 — the bulldozer's force in the same direction as displacement increases stored ability.
Pattern 3 — the wind's force is perpendicular to the boulder's displacement, so it cannot change stored ability.
Pattern 5 — greater displacement stores more ability, so the wind needs to push further.

questions.stem: A diver pushes a crate upward toward the surface. The crate moves upward and its stored potential ability increases. Which pattern best explains this?
options: Pattern 2 — force opposite to displacement decreases stored ability.
Pattern 1 — the external system exerts force in the same direction as displacement, increasing stored potential ability.
Pattern 3 — perpendicular force cannot change stored ability.
Pattern 4 — stronger force over the same displacement stores more ability.

questions.stem: A guitar player stretches a string by pulling it farther than usual before releasing it. The string snaps back with more force than a lightly pulled string would. Which pattern explains why pulling farther stores more potential ability in the string?
options: Pattern 4 — a stronger force over the same displacement stores more ability.
Pattern 3 — perpendicular force fails to change stored ability.
Pattern 5 — the same force applied over a greater displacement stores more potential ability.
Pattern 2 — force opposite to displacement decreases stored ability.

questions.stem: A space probe is slowed by a retro-rocket firing opposite to its direction of travel for 200 m. The stored ability of the probe system decreases. Which pattern best explains this?
options: Pattern 1 — force in the same direction as displacement increases stored ability.
Pattern 5 — the same force over greater displacement stores more ability.
Pattern 2 — an external system exerting force opposite to displacement decreases the system's stored ability.
Pattern 3 — perpendicular force cannot change stored ability.

questions.stem: An astronaut pushes two crates. Crate A is pushed with 200 N for 5 m. Crate B is pushed with 400 N for 5 m. Both forces are in the direction of motion. Crate B stores more ability. Which pattern explains this?
options: Pattern 5 — the same force over greater displacement stores more ability.
Pattern 3 — perpendicular force cannot change stored ability.
Pattern 4 — when displacement is the same, a stronger force stores more ability in the system.
Pattern 2 — force opposite to displacement decreases stored ability.

questions.stem: A student tries to push open a heavy bank vault door. The door doesn't move. Despite pushing hard for 2 minutes, the vault system's stored ability is unchanged. Which pattern best explains this outcome?
options: Pattern 1 — force in the same direction as displacement increases stored ability.
Pattern 4 — stronger force over the same displacement stores more ability.
Pattern 2 — force opposite to displacement decreases stored ability.
Pattern 3 — an external system cannot change stored ability if the system cannot be displaced.

questions.stem: A piano hammer strikes a string, pushing it downward in the same direction the string initially moves. The string's stored potential ability increases immediately after the strike. Which pattern explains this?
options: Pattern 3 — perpendicular force cannot change stored ability.
Pattern 2 — force opposite to displacement decreases stored ability.
Pattern 1 — the external system exerts force in the same direction as displacement, increasing the system's stored potential ability.
Pattern 5 — greater displacement stores more ability.

questions.stem: A crane lowers a beam by letting it descend while pushing upward on it (the beam moves down, the crane pushes up). The beam's stored potential ability decreases as it descends. Which pattern best explains this?
options: Pattern 1 — force in the same direction as displacement increases stored ability.
Pattern 2 — the external system exerts force opposite to the direction of displacement, decreasing the system's stored potential ability.
Pattern 4 — stronger force over the same displacement stores more ability.
Pattern 3 — perpendicular force cannot change stored ability.

modifiers.label: Resonant Strike — consecutive correct answers amplify your output
Stored Reserves — correct answers restore a fragment of your HP

phases.name: Coiled Patience
phases.dialogue: The Sovereign absorbs your assault without flinching. It is waiting. It is storing.

phases.name: Sovereign Release
phases.dialogue: YOU HAVE COMPRESSED ME ENOUGH. NOW I RELEASE WHAT YOU GAVE ME — EVERY NEWTON, EVERY METER, ALL OF IT.

bossAbilities.name: Elastic Discharge
bossAbilities.description: The Sovereign releases stored ability in a violent burst — all active operatives take shockwave damage as the system equalizes.

bossAbilities.name: Maximum Compression
bossAbilities.description: Pushed past its equilibrium point, the Sovereign enters a state of maximum stored potential. Its retaliatory force surges for the next 4 questions.

bossAbilities.name: Displacement Lock
bossAbilities.description: The Sovereign identifies the operative dealing the most damage and redirects its release force directly at them.
